title: Multimode Strong-Coupling Processes in Circuit QED Lattices
authors: Won Chan Lee, Ali Fahimniya, Kellen O'Brien, Yu-Xin Wang, Alexandra Behne, Maya Amouzegar, Alexey V. Gorshkov, Alicia J. Kollár
abstract: Circuit QED systems provide an ideal platform for exploring the strong-coupling regime of multimode cavity QED. Here we present two new phenomena from multimode strong coupling: a circuit Lagrangian analysis which captures beyond tight-binding effects of strong photon-photon coupling and experimental observation of strong wave-mixing resonances in the qubit response. Our circuit analysis reveals qualitatively new features such as emergent band gaps, lifted degeneracies, broadened flat bands, and frequency-dependent hopping. Within the multimode photon environment, strong qubit-photon coupling in turn gives rise to multiphoton processes involving multiple normal modes. We demonstrate a strong four-wave-mixing process involving excitation of a qubit and simultaneous frequency conversion between modes. Notably, this wave-mixing process is dominated by localized flat-band modes of the photonic lattice, which exhibit the strongest coupling to the transmon qubit.
summary: This paper explores the physics of multimode strong coupling in a circuit QED lattice, revealing new band structure features like emergent gaps and frequency-dependent hopping. It experimentally demonstrates a four-wave-mixing process that is uniquely driven by localized flat-band modes. The work bridges the gap between circuit-level dynamics and effective many-body lattice models.
main_problem: Investigating phenomena arising from multimode strong coupling in circuit QED lattices, specifically addressing the limitations of standard tight-binding models and identifying new nonlinear processes like four-wave mixing.
main_result: The study reveals that beyond-tight-binding effects lead to emergent band gaps and frequency-dependent hopping, and experimentally demonstrates a strong four-wave-mixing process dominated by localized flat-band modes.
method: A combination of a circuit Lagrangian analysis using a lumped-element approach and experimental two-tone spectroscopy (pump-probe) on a superconducting circuit lattice.
model_or_system: A quasi-1D superconducting coplanar-waveguide (CPW) lattice consisting of nine unit cells with six resonators per cell, incorporating flux-tunable transmon qubits.
key_observables: Density of States (DoS), band structure evolution, four-wave-mixing resonances, qubit population (via IQ distance), and AC Stark shifts.
important_parameters: Strong-coupling regime, hopping parameter t (2.5 to 250 MHz), qubit frequency (approx. 9.15 GHz), and transmon anharmonicity (-125 MHz).
main_assumptions: The tight-binding model is valid only for small hopping strengths; higher CPW harmonics are excluded as they lie well above the qubit operating frequencies.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows simulated DoS and band evolution with varying hopping; Figure S1 shows the device photo and band structure; Figure S7 shows two-tone spectroscopy with qubit transitions and FWM resonances.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the multimode strong-coupling problem, presents a circuit Lagrangian analysis to identify new band features, describes the experimental CPW lattice platform, details the observation of four-wave-mixing, and provides a theoretical validation of the FWM mechanism and symmetry.
why_interesting: It provides a deep look into how strong coupling in a multi-mode environment breaks standard approximations (like tight-binding or RWA) and demonstrates how localized flat-band modes can be used to mediate complex nonlinear photon-photon interactions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Simulated Density of States of the Full-Wave Modes versus Nearest-Neighbor Hopping t. Density of states (DoS) as a function of energy E and hopping t (a) in a fixed energy window for all t on a logarithmic scale and (b) in an energy window that scales linearly with t. The energy range used in (b) is indicated in (a) by dashed black lines. The inset in (a) shows the resonator connectivity of the device. (c)-(d) The line cuts of (a) and (b), respectively, are shown at three representative values of t (colored horizontal lines in the corresponding 2D maps). Flat bands are marked by black stars in (b) and (d). In the simulations, we set M = 100.

Figure 2. Four-Wave Mixing in a Quasi-1D Lattice. (a) Qubit population from continuous-wave measurement exhibits three features: the g −e transition at ωq = 2π × 9.15 GHz, the (g −f)/2 (two-photon) transition at 2π × 9.087 GHz, and an additional spectral response at ω∗ p = 2π × 9.211 GHz corresponding to the four-wave-mixing process. The inset shows the lowest three transmon energy levels, illustrating the negative anharmonicity −α/2π = −125 MHz. (b) Schematic of the four-wave-mixing process. Two input photons—the monitor photon at ωm and the pump photon at ω∗ p—combine to generate two outputs: a qubit photon ωq and the undriven output photon at ωw. The diagram on the right shows all...

Figure 3. Four-Wave-Mixing Characterization. (a) Qubit population extracted for different monitor modes as a function of ωp. The monitor mode frequencies are indicated in the legend. The qubit frequencies ωq = 2π × 9.15 GHz and the (g −f)/2 transition remain constant. The pump frequencies ω∗ p where the wave-mixing process is resonant are marked by colored arrows. (b)–(c) The resonant pump frequencies ω∗ p and calculated wave-mixing frequencies ωw versus (b) ωm and (c) ωq. In both cases, the output frequency calculated from Eq.
